sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Gait aid assessment aims to improve safety, confidence, and load sharing without creating unnecessary dependence or unsafe technique.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Reduced balance, pain, weakness, or fatigue limiting safe walking
- Recent falls or near-falls
- Need for temporary offloading after injury or surgery
- Difficulty walking in camp, stairs, uneven terrain, or longer distances

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- The wrong gait aid or poor sizing can worsen falls risk
- Cane, crutch, frame, and rollator choice should match the actual deficit and environment
- Not every patient who is unsteady is suitable for independent gait-aid use without supervision

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Decide whether the patient currently needs supervised support, a temporary aid, or urgent escalation
- Check height, hand placement, and whether the patient can safely use the device over real terrain
- Escalate if even assisted walking remains unsafe

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Match the aid to the patient's strength, cognition, endurance, and environment
- Train turning, stairs, sit-to-stand, and uneven-ground use where relevant
- Reassess whether the aid is still required as pain, balance, and load tolerance improve

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - A gait aid is only useful if the patient can actually use it safely in the places they live and work
